section.title: The Sealed Agreement
section.sectionRationale: Flowing straight out of the great confession of chapter nine, the people now put their commitment in writing. A long roll of names, the governor, the priests, the Levites, and the leaders of the people, is fixed to a sealed document; then the whole community binds itself with a sworn oath to keep God's law in the concrete details of life: marriage, Sabbath, the seventh year, and the ongoing support of the temple through tax, wood, firstfruits, firstborn, and tithes. It is one movement in which repentance takes the shape of a signed, specific covenant.
section.thesis: Genuine repentance does not stop at feeling; it commits itself in writing and in the specifics of ordinary life, so the renewed people bind themselves by oath to keep the Law and, above all, resolve that they will not neglect the house of their God.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the governor and priests who sealed the document; the Levites and leaders who joined them; the binding oath against mixed marriage and for the Sabbath and seventh year; the temple tax and the wood offering; and firstfruits, firstborn, and tithes, closing on the vow not to neglect the house of God.

units.label: The governor and the priests seal the document
units.note: these were the ones who sealed

verses.text: Now these were the ones who sealed the document: Nehemiah the governor, son of Hacaliah, and also Zedekiah,
Maaziah, Bilgai, and Shemaiah. These were the priests.

units.label: The Levites and the leaders of the people join them
units.note: the covenant belongs to the whole community

verses.text: The Levites: Jeshua son of Azaniah, Binnui of the sons of Henadad, Kadmiel,
and their associates: Shebaniah, Hodiah, Kelita, Pelaiah, Hanan,
And the leaders of the people: Parosh, Pahath-moab, Elam, Zattu, Bani,

units.label: The binding oath: marriage, Sabbath, and the seventh year
units.note: a sworn oath to follow the Law of God

verses.text: “The rest of the people, the priests, Levites, gatekeepers, singers, temple servants, and all who had separated themselves from the people of the land to obey the Law of God, along with their wives and all their sons and daughters who are able to understand,

hereby join with their noble brothers and commit themselves with a sworn {{alah|oath}} to follow the Law of God given through His servant Moses and to obey carefully all the commandments, ordinances, and statutes of the LORD our Lord.
We will not give our daughters in marriage to the people of the land, and we will not take their daughters for our sons.

When the people of the land bring merchandise or any kind of grain to sell on the Sabbath day, we will not buy from them on a Sabbath or holy day. Every seventh year we will let the fields lie fallow and will cancel every debt.

units.label: The temple tax and the wood offering
units.note: obligation for the service of God's house

verses.text: We also place ourselves under the obligation to contribute a third of a shekel yearly for the service of the {{beth-elohenu|house of our God}}:

for the showbread, for the regular grain offerings and burnt offerings, for the Sabbath offerings, for the New Moons and appointed feasts, for the holy offerings, for the sin offerings to make atonement for Israel, and for all the duties of the house of our God.

We have cast lots among the priests, Levites, and people for the donation of wood by our families at the appointed times each year. They are to bring it to the house of our God to burn on the altar of the LORD our God, as it is written in the Law.

units.label: Firstfruits, firstborn, and tithes
units.note: we will not neglect the house of our God

verses.text: We will also bring the {{bikkurim|firstfruits}} of our land and of every fruit tree to the house of the LORD year by year.

And we will bring the firstborn of our sons and our livestock, as it is written in the Law, and will bring the firstborn of our herds and flocks to the house of our God, to the priests who minister in the house of our God.

Moreover, we will bring to the priests at the storerooms of the house of our God the firstfruits of our dough, of our grain offerings, of the fruit of all our trees, and of our new wine and oil. A tenth of our produce belongs to the Levites, so that they shall receive tithes in all the towns where we labor.
A priest of Aaron’s line is to accompany the Levites when they collect the tenth, and the Levites are to bring a tenth of these tithes to the storerooms of the treasury in the house of our God.

For the Israelites and the Levites are to bring the contributions of grain, new wine, and oil to the storerooms where the articles of the sanctuary are kept and where the ministering priests, the gatekeepers, and the singers stay. Thus we will not neglect the house of our God.”

alah.gloss: A solemn oath that carries a self-invoked curse if broken. The people do not merely resolve to do better; they bind themselves under oath, calling down covenant sanctions on themselves if they fail. It shows how seriously they took obedience, and how weighty a vow before God really is.

beth-elohenu.gloss: The house of our God, the temple. This phrase drums through the chapter as the covenant's center of gravity; nearly every commitment, tax, wood, firstfruits, firstborn, and tithe, exists so that worship at God's house will not fail. The whole oath ends on the resolve not to neglect it.

bikkurim.gloss: Firstfruits, the first and best of the harvest, brought to the LORD before the rest is used. To give God the first, not the leftovers, was to confess that all the produce, and the giver, belong to Him. It anticipates Christ, called the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.

basic.context: After the confession and worship of chapter nine, the people put their repentance in writing. Nehemiah the governor, then the priests, the Levites, and the leaders of the people, set their seal to a formal document. Then the whole community, down to the wives and children old enough to understand, binds itself with a sworn oath to keep the Law of Moses. The oath then gets specific: no intermarriage with the idolatrous peoples of the land, no buying and selling on the Sabbath, keeping the seventh-year rest and debt release, and providing for the temple through a yearly tax, wood for the altar, firstfruits, firstborn, and tithes. It all ends on one ringing line: we will not neglect the house of our God.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative that shows repentance becoming concrete. The particular laws here, the Sabbath market, the temple tax, the wood offering, belong to Israel under the old covenant and are not rules to transfer directly to us. What endures is the pattern: real turning to God shapes marriage, money, work, rest, and worship, not just feelings. The ban on intermarriage is about spiritual fidelity, not race; the danger was being pulled back into idolatry.
basic.originalLanguages: The people commit themselves with an alah, a sworn oath that calls down a curse on covenant-breakers, which is why this is no light promise. The phrase beth elohenu, the house of our God, echoes again and again as the heart of the whole agreement. And they pledge the bikkurim, the firstfruits, the first and best of the harvest, given to God before anything else.
basic.typology: The oath to keep the whole Law, which Israel could never fully perform, points ahead to the one who does keep it perfectly. The firstfruits given to God point to Christ, the firstfruits of the resurrection. And the endless temple offerings for atonement point to the single, final sacrifice He would make.
basic.christ: This chapter shows a people binding themselves by oath to keep a law they will, in fact, break again by the end of the book. That very failure drives the story toward Christ, the one who keeps the law we cannot, and who, under the new covenant, writes it not on a sealed scroll but on the heart. Their care that the house of God not be neglected finds its answer in Him, who is Himself the true temple and the final sacrifice.
basic.god: God is worthy of an obedience that reaches into every corner of life, and He graciously receives a people who commit themselves to Him. Yet the chapter also quietly exposes the limit of human resolve: sincere vows are not the same as a changed heart. God is the one who must ultimately keep His people faithful.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 31 promises a new covenant written on the heart. Hebrews 8 declares that new covenant arrived in Christ. Deuteronomy 7 gives the command against intermarriage with idolaters that stands behind verse 30. And 1 Corinthians 15 names Christ the firstfruits.
basic.oneStory: Repentance takes visible shape here in a signed, specific covenant to keep God's law and support His house. But the very solemnity of the oath sets up the deeper need: a people who cannot keep their word need a Savior who keeps His, and a new covenant that writes the law within. The story runs from this sealed scroll to the cross, where Christ secures the obedience the people could only promise.

context.literary: The chapter is the practical outworking of the confession in chapter nine, moving from prayer to a signed pledge. It falls into two halves: a long name list of those who sealed the covenant, and the covenant's specific terms, framed by an opening summary in verse 28 and closing on the memorable vow of verse 39. The repeated phrase the house of our God binds the terms together.
context.historical: In the small, fragile post-exilic community, the temple's ongoing worship depended on the people's regular giving, and the surrounding peoples exerted constant pressure toward compromise in marriage, trade, and religion. A sealed document with named signatories was a recognized legal act, making the community's commitment public, binding, and formally witnessed before God.
context.canonical: This covenant renewal stands in a long line of such moments, from Sinai to Joshua to Josiah, and looks ahead to the new covenant promised in Jeremiah and fulfilled in Christ. Its concern to keep Israel distinct and its house of worship supplied serves the larger story: preserving the people through whom the Messiah would come.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record how the renewed community turned confession into a binding, specific covenant, sealed by its leaders and sworn by the whole people, and to fix in memory the concrete commitments, especially the resolve not to neglect the house of God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Israel's particular old-covenant obligations, the Sabbath market, the third-of-a-shekel tax, the wood offering, which are not binding on Christians today. What endures is the principle that genuine repentance reshapes marriage, money, rest, and worship, and that God's house and worship deserve deliberate, ordered, generous support.

debates.question: Was the ban on intermarriage in verse 30 about race or about faith?

views.label: Spiritual fidelity, not ethnicity
views.case: The concern throughout Ezra and Nehemiah is the peoples of the land whose idolatry had drawn Israel away before; foreigners who embraced the LORD, like Ruth the Moabitess, were fully welcomed. The issue is being bound to unbelief and its gods, not ethnic purity.

views.label: Preserving the distinct covenant people
views.case: There is also real concern to keep the returned remnant separate and consecrated, a people set apart to God through whom the promise would come; yet even this separateness was defined by covenant loyalty rather than by blood.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the New Testament principle of not being unequally yoked with unbelief, and both serve the coming of Christ, in whom people of every nation are gathered by faith.

debates.question: How should we read a solemn oath to keep the whole Law?

views.label: A sincere and commendable act of repentance
views.case: The people are responding rightly to God's word and grace; binding themselves to obedience in the specifics of life is exactly what true repentance looks like, and Scripture commends it.

views.label: A revealing of the limits of human resolve
views.case: By the end of Nehemiah the very commitments made here are broken again, showing that vows, however sincere, cannot by themselves produce a faithful heart; the episode exposes the need for a deeper, inward covenant.

debates.bearing: Held together, the two point forward: the pledge is good and right, yet it drives the story toward the new covenant in which God writes His law on the heart and Christ keeps it on our behalf.

study.originalLanguages: The people commit themselves with an alah, not a casual promise but a sworn oath that calls a curse down on the one who breaks it; this is covenant-making at its most solemn, obedience sealed under sanction. What the whole document circles is the beth elohenu, the house of our God, a phrase that recurs so often it becomes the drumbeat of the chapter; tax, wood, firstfruits, firstborn, and tithe are all ordered toward keeping worship there alive, and the oath climaxes on the vow not to neglect it. Among those gifts are the bikkurim, the firstfruits, the first and best of field and tree, brought to God before the harvest is touched, a confession that everything, and everyone, belongs to Him. Oath, house of God, firstfruits: the vocabulary of a people trying to give God not the leftovers of life but its center and its first.

typology.type: The oath to keep the whole Law of Moses
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who alone keeps the Law perfectly and fulfills it on our behalf, Matthew 5:17 and Romans 8:3-4.

typology.type: The firstfruits brought to the house of God
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, the pledge of the full harvest of resurrection, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.

typology.type: The temple offerings and sin offerings that make atonement
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the single final sacrifice who ends the endless offerings, Hebrews 10:11-14.

typology.type: The law sealed on an outward document
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant in which God writes His law on the heart, Jeremiah 31:33 and Hebrews 8:10.

study.christ: The whole scene is a people binding themselves by oath to keep God's law, down to the details of marriage, Sabbath, and giving. It is sincere and good, and yet by the last chapter of Nehemiah these very promises are broken again, the Sabbath violated, the tithes withheld, the mixed marriages returning. That failure is not the end of the story but a signpost. It shows that a sealed scroll and a solemn vow cannot make a heart faithful. The answer comes in Jesus, who keeps the law we could not, who is Himself the true house of God and the final sacrifice that all those temple offerings pointed toward, and who inaugurates the new covenant Jeremiah promised, writing the law not on a document but within His people. Their trembling care that the house of God not be neglected is honored and surpassed in Him.
study.god: God is worthy of obedience that reaches every part of life, marriage, work, rest, money, and worship, and He graciously receives a people who solemnly give themselves to Him. Yet the same chapter, read alongside the rest of the book, reveals something about God's people and so about their need of God: sincere resolve is not a new heart, and vows alone cannot secure faithfulness. The God who calls for this covenant is the God who must finally keep His people in it, which He does by grace, through the new covenant in Christ. He is both the one worthy of our first and best and the one who supplies the faithfulness we lack.

crossReferences.note: The promise of a new covenant in which God writes His law on the heart, answering the limits of an oath sealed on a scroll.
Declares that new and better covenant established in Christ, surpassing the old.
The command against intermarriage with idolatrous peoples that stands behind the pledge of verse 30.
Christ the firstfruits of the resurrection, fulfilling the firstfruits brought to God's house.
The charge of robbing God by withholding tithes and offerings, the very failure this covenant sought to prevent.

study.oneStory: Repentance here takes concrete shape: a sealed document, named signatories, and a sworn oath to keep God's law in the specifics of marriage, Sabbath, and generous support of His house. But the solemn vow sets up the deeper need the whole Bible answers, for a people who cannot keep their word require a Savior who keeps His. The story runs from this scroll, soon broken, to the new covenant in Christ, who keeps the law on our behalf, writes it on the heart, and is Himself the true temple that will never be neglected.

sermon.title: Renewing the Covenant for the Sake of God's Name
sermon.why: Piper preaches on the covenant reaffirmation and the sealed document with the names of leaders, Levites, and priests, the very act recorded in Nehemiah 10, showing that such a covenant is only possible because God is abundantly gracious and steadfast in love. A fitting, gospel-centered handling of this movement's sealed agreement.

quiz.q: What did the people do first as the fruit of their confession?

options.text: They put their commitment in writing, and their leaders sealed a formal document.
options.note: Verses 1-27 record the governor, priests, Levites, and leaders sealing the covenant.

options.text: They immediately rebuilt the temple from the ground up.
options.note: The temple already stood; the fresh act here was sealing a written covenant, not rebuilding.

options.text: They appointed a new king over Judah.
options.note: There was no new king; Judah was under Persian rule and led by Nehemiah the governor.

options.text: They abandoned the Law of Moses.
options.note: The opposite; they bound themselves by oath to obey the Law of Moses.

quiz.q: What kind of commitment did the people make in verse 29?

options.text: A sworn oath, binding themselves under God to follow the Law given through Moses.
options.note: The Hebrew alah is a solemn oath that carries a self-invoked curse on those who break it.

options.text: A casual, non-binding suggestion to try harder.
options.note: It was far more than a suggestion; it was a formal, sworn, binding oath.

options.text: A promise binding only on the priests.
options.note: The oath included the whole community, down to wives and children old enough to understand.

options.text: A vow to keep only the laws they found convenient.
options.note: They swore to obey carefully all the commandments, ordinances, and statutes.

quiz.q: Why did the covenant forbid marriage with the peoples of the land?

options.text: To guard against being drawn back into their idolatry, the very sin that had led to exile.
options.note: The concern was spiritual fidelity; foreigners who embraced the LORD, like Ruth, were welcomed.

options.text: To keep the bloodline ethnically pure for its own sake.
options.note: The issue was covenant loyalty, not race; the danger was idolatry, not ethnicity.

options.text: Because marriage itself was regarded as unclean.
options.note: Marriage was honored; the danger was being unequally yoked to idol worship.

options.text: Because Persian law required it.
options.note: It flowed from God's command in the Law, not from Persian law.

quiz.q: What single resolve does the covenant end on in verse 39?

options.text: We will not neglect the house of our God.
options.note: The whole oath climaxes on this vow to keep the temple and its worship supplied.

options.text: We will build a wall around every city in Judah.
options.note: The wall was built earlier; the covenant's closing vow concerns the house of God, not walls.

options.text: We will conquer the surrounding nations.
options.note: There is no pledge of conquest; the focus is faithful worship and giving.

options.text: We will never again offer any sacrifices.
options.note: The opposite; they committed to sustaining the offerings at the temple.

quiz.q: How does this sealed covenant point us toward Christ?

options.text: It shows a people who cannot finally keep their oath, driving the story toward the one who keeps the law and writes it on the heart.
options.note: By the end of Nehemiah the vows are broken, revealing the need for the new covenant in Christ, Jeremiah 31 and Hebrews 8.

options.text: It proves that sincere vows alone are enough to make a heart faithful.
options.note: The book shows the opposite; the very commitments here are later broken.

options.text: It teaches that God no longer cares about obedience.
options.note: God cares deeply about obedience; the point is that Christ secures what we cannot.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ or the gospel.
options.note: The firstfruits, the sacrifices, and the promised law-keeper all point to Christ.

questions: The people turned their confession into something written and specific. Where is God calling you to move from vague good intentions to concrete, named commitments?
Their oath reached into marriage, money, work, and rest, not just their feelings. Which ordinary corner of your life most needs to be brought under God's lordship right now?
They gave God the firstfruits, the first and best, rather than the leftovers. What would it look like for God to have the first of your time, energy, and money instead of what remains?
The whole covenant circled around not neglecting the house of God. How are you tempted to let the worship and life of God's people slip down your list of priorities?
By the end of the book these very vows were broken. How does knowing that Christ keeps the law we cannot, and writes it on our hearts, change the way you pursue obedience this week?

translationNotes.note: In the Hebrew text the verse numbering differs; what English Bibles print as Nehemiah 9:38 begins the covenant here, so some editions start chapter 10 one verse earlier. The content, the sealed document and its signatories, is the same.
The temple servants render the Hebrew Nethinim, a class of assistants attached to the temple; the BSB footnote gives the Hebrew term.

A third of a shekel is roughly 3.8 grams, probably of silver; the BSB footnote gives the weight. It differs from the half-shekel of Exodus 30, likely reflecting the economic strain of the small post-exilic community.
Cancel every debt renders the seventh-year release of Deuteronomy 15; together with letting the fields lie fallow it keeps the sabbatical-year law that Israel had long ignored.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Nehemiah 10:1-39. All 39 verses present in order. Em dashes in verse 28 converted to commas; no em or en dashes present or introduced. Sermon verified this session: John Piper, Desiring God, on the covenant reaffirmation and sealed document of Nehemiah 9:38 to 10.
